meta.title: P38A Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Range Rover P38A engine — M51 diesel, 4.0 V8, 4.6 V8. Real UK costs, liner-drop risk explained, and classic car buying advice. Get free quotes from 12+ vetted Land Rover specialists today.

@graph.name: Land Rover Range Rover P38A Engine Codes
Land Rover Range Rover P38A Core Variants

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover P38A engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned P38A engine typically costs £1,800–£5,500 supply only, depending on engine — the 2.5-litre diesel sits at £1,800–£3,500, while the 4.6 V8 can reach £3,000–£5,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,040. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Land Rover Range Rover P38A engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The BMW M51 2.5-litre turbodiesel is generally the most reliable P38A engine when maintained — it's a simpler unit than the Rover V8s and avoids the liner-drop risk entirely. However, it's ageing and the injection pump is a known weak point.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Range Rover P38A?

acceptedAnswer.text: Porous cylinder blocks and dropped liners on the Rover V8s (4.0 and 4.6), overheating and head gasket failure on all V8s, injection pump failure on the BMW M51 diesel, and — unrelated to the engine — the air suspension compressor and EAS system are this generation's defining non-engine headache.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Range Rover P38A reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Reliability is mixed and highly dependent on engine choice and maintenance history. The BMW diesel is the most dependable option, but it's an ageing unit. The Rover V8s are characterful but carry significant risk of block failure. A well-maintained P38A can be a rewarding classic, but it demands careful inspection and a healthy budget for repairs.

mainEntity.name: Which Land Rover Range Rover P38A engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 4.6 V8 without documented history of a top-hat liner rebuild — it has the thinnest liners and the highest risk of catastrophic failure. Any P38A V8 with signs of overheating or coolant loss should be approached with extreme caution.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my P38A engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Overheating with no obvious external leak (V8 block porosity), persistent coolant loss, white smoke from the exhaust (head gasket), or rattling on cold start and poor running (diesel injection pump) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Land Rover Range Rover P38A engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £8,000 P38A, a £4,000 V8 recon is 50% of the car's value — borderline but justifiable. Against a tired £4,000 example, the same recon is 100% — walk away. Our 60% Land Rover threshold is the line. A top-hat-liner rebuild adds significant value to a V8.

mainEntity.name: How long do Land Rover Range Rover P38A engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M51 diesel can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The Rover V8s can also reach high mileage — but only if they've been rebuilt with top-hat liners. Without this work, the 4.0 and 4.6 rarely exceed 150,000 miles before block issues appear.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the Range Rover P38A and the L322?

acceptedAnswer.text: The P38A is the second-generation Range Rover (1994–2002) with a semi-monocoque chassis and Rover V8s or BMW diesel. The L322 is the third-generation (2002–2012) with a BMW E65-derived platform, a wider engine range (including BMW V8s and TDV6s) and significantly more electronics. The two generations are not interchangeable and have different engine fitments.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Land Rover Range Rover P38A engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible on the engine itself. For the Rover V8s, the engine code is cast into the block near the starter motor. Tell us your variant (4.0 V8, 4.6 V8, 2.5 TD) and we'll identify it — 12+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Land Rover Range Rover P38A Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the second-generation Range Rover P38A — from the Rover V8s to the BMW-sourced M51 turbodiesel. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the V8 liner-drop risk that defines this generation.

engineDatabase.h2: Land Rover Range Rover P38A Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the P38A — from the 4.0-litre and 4.6-litre Rover V8s to the BMW 2.5-litre six-cylinder turbodiesel.

overview.h2: Land Rover Range Rover P38A — Overview
overview.intro: The P38A is the second-generation Range Rover, launched on 28 September 1994 as the successor to the original Range Rover Classic. Built on a new semi-monocoque chassis with air suspension, it introduced a BMW-sourced 2.5-litre turbodiesel alongside updated versions of the Rover V8. Production ran until 2001–2002, with over 167,000 units built.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: P38A semi-monocoque • Predecessor: Range Rover Classic • Successor: Range Rover L322 • Sibling body styles in this generation: 5-door SUV only
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: As a classic model now over 20 years old, the P38A is a niche enthusiast vehicle — enquiry patterns are sparse, but the Rover V8s remain the most discussed engines in owner communities.

items.quote: The P38A's engine range is defined by two very different characters. The BMW M51 2.5-litre turbodiesel is the most economical choice and, when maintained, can be dependable — but it's a hard-worked unit that can suffer from injection pump failures and cracked heads. The Rover V8s (4.0 and 4.6) are the heart of the P38A experience — smooth, torquey and characterful — but they carry this generation's defining risk: porous or cracked cylinder blocks and dropped liners, particularly on the 4.6-litre. If you're buying a P38A, budget for a thorough engine inspection — and walk away from any V8 without documented cooling system history.

ownershipEconomics.h2: Land Rover Range Rover P38A — Ownership Economics

rows.ownershipVerdict: Solid Choice — economical but ageing BMW unit

rows.commonMajorFailure: Porous block, dropped liners, head gasket
rows.ownershipVerdict: High Risk — V8 character with V8 bills
Most Expensive — the 4.6 is the riskiest of the V8s

economicsRule.title: The P38A rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,000–£5,500 4.6 V8 recon against a £5,000 P38A sits at 60–110% of the car's value — right at our 60% Land Rover threshold. On a sub-£4,000 P38A, a V8 replacement is a financial write-off. Against a well-kept £8,000+ example, the same recon drops to 38–69% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which P38A Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M51D25 diesel — simpler fuel system than the V8s, better economy.

keyTakeaways.question: Which P38A Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 4.6 V8 — liner drops and overheating repairs add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which P38A Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: 4.0 V8 with documented cooling history — cheaper to buy than the 4.6, easier to find parts for.

problems.title: Rover V8 Porous Block & Dropped Liners
problems.rootCause: The Rover V8's thin cylinder walls can develop porosity or stress fractures from heat cycling — and the liners can drop, leading to coolant loss, overheating, and catastrophic engine failure. The 4.6-litre is particularly prone due to its thinner liners and more aggressive mapping.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a P38A V8, pay for a specialist inspection that includes a cooling system pressure test and block check. The single most important question is: "Has the engine been rebuilt with top-hat liners?" If the answer is no, budget for it — or walk away.

cta.label: Compare P38A V8 engine prices for your Range Rover →

problems.title: Rover V8 Overheating & Head Gasket Failure
problems.rootCause: The thermostat can fail closed, causing the engine to overheat rapidly. The aluminium V8 is sensitive to heat — once overheated, head gaskets fail, and the block can warp. Core plugs can also leak, compounding the issue.
problems.recommendation: Replace the thermostat proactively on any P38A V8 without documented history — it's a £200–£400 job that can save you from a £2,500+ head gasket bill. Monitor the temperature gauge obsessively.

cta.label: Compare P38A V8 cooling system parts for your Range Rover →

problems.title: BMW M51 Diesel Injection Pump & Crankshaft Issues
problems.rootCause: The BMW M51's mechanical injection pump has a limited service life and can fail, causing poor starting, rough running, and eventual non-starting. The in-tank lift pump is also prone to failure. Less common but more serious: the timing chain tensioner can fail if the engine is turned backwards.
problems.recommendation: The M51 is the most economical P38A to run, but it's an ageing BMW diesel with known weak points. If the engine struggles to start or runs lumpy, suspect the injection pump or lift pump first. Replace the lift pump proactively — it's a cheap fix that can save the injection pump.

cta.label: Compare P38A M51 engine prices for your Range Rover →
